---
title: "Essay: Understanding Shiny Apps"
---

## What is a Shiny App?

Shiny is an R package that lets you build interactive web applications directly from R code — no need to know HTML, CSS, or JavaScript. If you can write R, you can build a working web app. This is especially useful for anyone doing data analysis who wants to let others explore that data themselves, rather than just handing over a static report.

## The Two Core Pieces: UI and Server

Every Shiny app is built from two main components:

**1. UI (User Interface)** This defines what the user sees — the layout, buttons, dropdowns, sliders, text boxes, and where outputs like plots or tables appear.

**2. Server** This defines what happens behind the scenes — the logic that takes user input, processes it, and generates the outputs shown in the UI.

A simple example looks like this:

``` r
library(shiny)

ui <- fluidPage(
  sliderInput("num", "Choose a number:", min = 1, max = 100, value = 50),
  textOutput("result")
)

server <- function(input, output) {
  output$result <- renderText({
    paste("You selected:", input$num)
  })
}

shinyApp(ui = ui, server = server)
```

## Reactivity: The Heart of Shiny

What makes Shiny powerful is **reactivity** — when a user changes an input (like moving a slider), Shiny automatically re-runs only the parts of the code that depend on that input, and updates the output in real time. You don't have to manually refresh anything; it just works.

## Why This Matters

Shiny apps are ideal when you want to:

-   Let non-technical stakeholders explore data themselves
-   Build internal tools without needing a full web development team
-   Turn a one-time analysis into something reusable and interactive

## Requirement: A Running Server

One important thing to understand about traditional Shiny apps: they need to run on a server. That means someone (you, your IT team, or a hosting service like shinyapps.io) has to keep an R process running in the background to power the app. This is important context for understanding Shinylive, covered in the next essay, which solves this exact limitation.
